text: The laparoscopic procedure begins with the temporary retraction of the uterus and sigmoid colon, providing clear access to the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum over the sacral promontory, exposing and freeing the anterior bony surface.
The surgeon continues to open the peritoneum, advancing towards the Douglas pouch, using laparoscopic instruments to carefully dissect the tissue.
The surgeon enters the vaginal septum and continues the dissection towards the pelvic floor, advancing the surgical field within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon navigates a virgin plane medial to the incorporated mesh, which remains untouched during the procedure.

The surgeon positions and folds the mesh over the posterior aspect of the rectum, securing it with multiple interrupted partial thickness PDS-TRIO sutures from the deepest point of dissection over the rectal mesentery.
The surgeon observes the Douglas pouch becoming less deep due to the suspension of the rectum, leading to excess peritoneum that is visible and can be resected.
The surgeon positions the anterior part of the mesh onto the deepest point of dissection, securing it onto the denuded anterior aspect of the rectum.
The surgeon brings both meshes together over the sacral promontory and securely fixes them in place, ensuring a snug fit.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum, effectively separating it from the abdominal cavity, completing the procedure.

The surgeon begins dissection over the sacral promontory, identifying a virgin plane and carefully dissecting left of the previous mesh. The dissection continues towards the rectovaginal septum, reaching the pelvic floor.
The surgical team performs additional posterior mobilization of the rectum due to the severity of the recurrent rectal prolapse.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the rectovaginal septum, carefully dissecting onto the pelvic floor to address the recurrent rectal prolapse.
The surgeon encounters a fibrous strand in the rectovaginal septum, a typical finding following previous dissection and mesh insertion.
The surgeon observes that the mesh appears to be fixed only onto the vaginal wall, rather than the rectum.
A retractor is placed in the vagina to provide additional space and traction for the ongoing dissection.
The surgeon operates deeper in the rectovaginal septum, navigating a virgin plane with no previous dissection evident.
The surgeon positions the mesh over the anterior aspect of the rectum and folds it, while suturing it in place to secure the mesh.
The surgeon secures the mesh onto the sacral promontory, completing the fixation process.
The surgeon resects a redundant portion of the mesh, removing excess material from the surgical field.
The surgeon fixes the vaginal top onto the mesh using PDS-TRIO sutures, followed by closing the peritoneum to cover the mesh.
The surgeon removes the fixation of the sigmoid colon and uterus, likely involving the detachment of previously placed sutures or mesh.
The surgical view reveals a deep Douglas pouch, which is visually assessed for potential enterocele contributing to obstructed defecation.
The surgeon begins dissection in the Douglas pouch, carefully opening the peritoneum to expose underlying structures.
The surgeon observes the rectal vaginal septum positioned adjacent to the previously inserted mesh on the anterior pelvic floor.
The surgeon dissects the posterior plane, focusing on the area where the external rectal prolapse is predominantly located.

The surgeon positions and folds a new mesh over the anterior aspect of the rectum, suturing it onto the rectum using BDS-TRIO sutures. The previously inserted mesh is visible adjacent to the new mesh, still attached to the vagina.
The surgeon tensions the mesh and secures it onto the promontory, completing the fixation process.

The surgeon checks the final positioning of the mesh on the rectum. Additional fixation is applied if needed, before closing the peritoneum to envelop the mesh in the retroperitoneal space, minimizing the risk of adhesion formation in the abdominal cavity.
